glossary.definition: The ability to acquire and apply knowledge or skills.
glossary.sentence: In this topic, you will find out what we understand by intelligence, how artificial intelligence is defined, and what is a fundamental approach to assessment of intelligence of a computer system. Nowadays, it is believed that humans, animals and even plants possess intelligence. Human intelligence is studied in-depth in many fields of science, such as philosophy, linguistics, neuroscience, etc.

glossary.definition: Deals with developing hardware and software solutions endowed with human-similar intellectual processes, such as the ability to reason, discover meaning, generalise, or learn from experience.
glossary.sentence: It is a part of computer science, and it also consists of many sub-fields such as robotics, evolutionary computations, intelligent agents, knowledge representation, and others.

glossary.definition: An approach to AI that involves agents making decisions based on achieving the best outcome or, when there is uncertainty, the best expected outcome.
glossary.sentence: Systems acting rationally are based on the approach of rational agents.

glossary.definition: An entity capable of perceiving its environment and acting within it.
glossary.sentence: In this context, an agent is any entity which is capable to perceive its environment and to act within it.

glossary.definition: An agent that acts to achieve the best outcome or, when there is uncertainty, the best expected outcome.
glossary.sentence: A rational agent is one that acts to achieve the best outcome or, when there is uncertainty, the best expected outcome.

glossary.definition: A fundamental test in AI intended to assess if a computer system possesses intelligence by determining whether a human tester can differentiate between answers given by an artificial intelligence and those of a human being.
glossary.sentence: The Turing test underlies systems that act humanly and it is also one of the fundamental tests in the field of artificial intelligence intended to assess if a computer system possesses intelligence.

glossary.definition: A version of the Turing test requiring additional capabilities including computer vision for perceiving objects and robotics for manipulating objects and movement, aimed at simulating human-like behavior more comprehensively.
glossary.sentence: The so called the Total Turing test brings forward two more requirements (Russell and Norvig, 2009) : the computer must have computer vision to perceive objects, and robotics to manipulate objects and move about.

glossary.definition: The capability of a system to understand, interpret, and generate human languages in a valuable way for communication with users during interactions such as the Turing test.
glossary.sentence: To pass the Turing test, the computer system must have the following capabilities (Russell and Norvig, 2009) : natural language processing for maintaining successful communication with the tester as the human being does; knowledge representation, in order to store information acquired before and during the test.

glossary.definition: The theory and practice of representing knowledge for computer systems.
glossary.sentence: It can be defined more precisely as an incorporation of full range formally described knowledge into an intelligent system with the aim of supporting intelligent performance of the system (Graham and Barrett, 1997).

glossary.definition: The process of using stored information by a system to answer questions and draw conclusions.
glossary.sentence: To pass the Turing test, the computer system must have the following capabilities (Russell and Norvig, 2009) : natural language processing for maintaining successful communication with the tester as the human being does; knowledge representation, in order to store information acquired before and during the test. Automated reasoning, in order to use the stored information for answering questions and drawing conclusions;

glossary.definition: A method of data analysis that automates analytical model building. It is a branch of artificial intelligence based on the idea that systems can learn from data, identify patterns and make decisions with minimal human intervention.
glossary.sentence: Tom Mitchell (1997) has provided a mathematical definition: “A computer program is said to learn from experience E with respect to some task T and some performance measure P, if its performance on T, as measured by P, improves with experience E.”

glossary.definition: A paradigm in AI that relies on symbolic representations and rule-based systems for problem-solving and reasoning, often using formal logic.
glossary.sentence: There are two main paradigms of thinking or sets of assumptions in the field of artificial intelligence: symbolic artificial intelligence and connectionist artificial intelligence.

glossary.definition: A paradigm in AI that uses neural networks and other models inspired by biological neural systems, focusing on learning from data through examples rather than explicit rules.
glossary.sentence: There are two main paradigms of thinking or sets of assumptions in the field of artificial intelligence: symbolic artificial intelligence and connectionist artificial intelligence.

glossary.definition: A type of artificial intelligence that is based on the view that intelligence is a set of abilities and is therefore concerned with developing solutions for some specific tasks such as chess playing, medical diagnosis, etc.
glossary.sentence: Narrow (weak) artificial intelligence , that is also called immature or limited artificial intelligence, is based on the view that intelligence is a set of abilities and is therefore concerned with developing solutions for some specific tasks such as chess playing, medical diagnosis, etc. ( Pennachin and Goertzel , 2007) .

glossary.definition: An approach treats intelligence as a general capability that can be copied/replicated.
glossary.sentence: Artificial general intelligence or, in another words, strong artificial intelligence, which is also called human-level intelligence (Russell and Norvig, 2020) , is an approach treats intelligence as a general capability that can be copied/replicated (Callan, 2003) .

glossary.definition: An intelligence that surpasses human capabilities.
glossary.sentence: Artificial super intelligence is an intelligence that surpasses human capabilities (Russell and Norvig, 2020) .

glossary.definition: A focus on a wide range of organisms, processes, and phenomena that occur at multiple spatial and temporal scales.
glossary.sentence: "It focuses on a wide range of organisms, processes, and phenomena that occur at multiple spatial and temporal scales" ( Floreano and Mattiussi, 2008).

glossary.definition: A paradigm under new artificial intelligence. It considers collective intelligence as a behaviour that emerges through the interaction and cooperation of large numbers of lesser intelligent agents.
glossary.sentence: Swarm intelligence is a paradigm under new artificial intelligence. It considers collective intelligence as a behaviour that emerges through the interaction and cooperation of large numbers of lesser intelligent agents.

glossary.definition: Systems designed to support humans in completing various functions and activities by resolving different types of problems.
glossary.sentence: However, this is not a trivial task, because to develop such systems we need to make a transition from the real world to the world of intelligent computer systems.

glossary.definition: A representation of all possible game states
glossary.sentence: It involves the following steps (Luger, 2009): create a complete state space graph; label the levels of the graph as MIN and MAX; assign heuristic measures to all leaf nodes according to an analysis of who wins at a specific node; propagate heuristic measures of leaf nodes up to the root node of the state space graph according to the following rules: a) if a node is located at MAX level, it acquires the maximum heuristic measure from among its direct descendants, and b) if a node is located at MIN level, it acquires the minimum heuristic measure from among its direct descendants; determine the result of the game and the winning paths.

glossary.definition: A structured approach to resolving issues, involving defining an initial state, identifying a goal state, determining if there is a discrepancy between these states, evaluating available resources and opportunities, selecting actions based on subjective criteria, and transitioning through intermediate states.
glossary.sentence: The structure indicates that you need to pass through a series of intermediate states to get from an initial state of the problem-solving process to a goal state.

glossary.definition: A set of values of properties for a given object or phenomenon at the specified time, representing the changing of states in a problem-solving process through performing actions.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, we can represent the problem-solving process as a structure showing states and transitions between them after performing actions. This structure indicates that you need to pass through a series of intermediate states to get from an initial state of the problem-solving process to a goal state.

glossary.definition: A step or operation performed in a problem-solving process, represented by arcs in the state space graph that show changes between states.
glossary.sentence: The transition from one state to another state takes place by carrying out some action. This structure is called a state space graph . The state space graph is a formal representation of a problem-solving process.

glossary.definition: A node in a state space graph representing the initial state or states of affairs of a problem-solving process.
glossary.sentence: The robot starts at location C1, which we can represent as a root node in the state space graph.

glossary.definition: A leaf node in the state space graph that corresponds to a goal state of a problem-solving process, which does not have any descendants.
glossary.sentence: The state space graph should have at least one terminal (leaf) node , which corresponds to a goal state of a problem-solving process. From the structural viewpoint, these are nodes that do not have descendants;

glossary.definition: A non-root and non-terminal node in the state space graph that corresponds to an intermediate state of a problem-solving process, which has both ancestors and descendants.
glossary.sentence: The state space graph may have internal nodes that correspond to intermediate states of a problem-solving process. From the structural viewpoint, these are nodes that have both ancestors and descendants;

glossary.definition: An edge in a state space graph representing an action that can be undertaken to solve a problem.
glossary.sentence: After that, humans determine resources and opportunities that together allow the identification of actions that can be undertaken to solve the problem. In the state space graph actions are represented by arcs, and together with intermediate states they define a course of problem-solving.

glossary.definition: A situation that needs to be resolved by defining an initial state, identifying a goal state, determining if there is a discrepancy between these states, evaluating available resources and opportunities, selecting actions based on subjective criteria, and transitioning through intermediate states.
glossary.sentence: To talk about the need to solve a problem, humans first need to become aware that the problem exists or, in other words, the problem needs to be defined. The definition of the problem means that we determine a state we are in at present (the so-called initial state of the problem-solving process ), a state we would like to reach (the so-called goal state of the problem-solving process ) and whether there is a discrepancy between these two states

glossary.definition: A node in a state space graph representing an intermediate state of a problem-solving process.
glossary.sentence: We represent an intermediate node C2 in the state space graph. Moreover, the robot can also turn to the left and go to location B1. This will be another intermediate node in the state space graph.

glossary.definition: A node in the search tree that has no child nodes.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm reaches a leaf node, which already has a heuristic measure

glossary.definition: A type of state space graph used to represent games and create an intelligent computer system that is able to play games against a human or another intelligent computer system.
glossary.sentence: This topic will explain to you how we can use a state space graph to represent games and thus to create an intelligent computer system that is able to play games against a human or against another intelligent computer system.

glossary.term: Zero-sum Deterministic Two-person Games with Perfect Information
glossary.definition: A type of game in which two players participate and compete against each other, make alternate moves until the end of the game, have access to complete information about the course of the game, involve no randomness factors, and one player's loss is equal to another player's gain.
glossary.sentence: This category of games includes both simple games such as tic-tac-toe and relatively complex ones such as chess, checkers, and Go .

glossary.definition: A game where the state space graph includes 362,880 nodes if all nodes are represented. By using symmetry features, this number can be reduced to 60,480 nodes.
glossary.sentence: In any case, a heuristic evaluation function is specific to a problem because each problem has specific heuristic knowledge associated with it.

glossary.definition: A systematic procedure for finding a solution in a state space graph by exploring its nodes and arcs.
glossary.sentence: The search algorithm is searching for a solution path in the state space graph you see in Figure 15.

glossary.definition: The initial node from which the search algorithm begins its exploration of the state space graph.
glossary.sentence: The search start node is A. The search goal node is M.

glossary.definition: A node in a state space graph that represents the desired outcome of a game, used by search-based game algorithms to determine when the algorithm has reached its objective.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm checks if the search goal node exists among the nodes located in the OPEN list. The search goal node is found; thus, the algorithm stops its operation.

glossary.definition: A sequence of nodes representing the actions taken by the robot to reach the search goal node in the state space graph.
glossary.sentence: One solution path is C1->B1->B2->B3, or we get a robot motion trajectory - the robot should move left-right-straight ahead.

glossary.definition: A node in the state space graph that has been generated but not yet expanded by the search algorithm.
glossary.sentence: As a result of this action, two types of nodes appear: open nodes – the search algorithm has already generated these nodes, but their descendants/ancestors have not yet been generated.

glossary.definition: A node in the state space graph that has been expanded by the search algorithm and its descendants or ancestors have already been generated.
glossary.sentence: As a result of this action, the set of open nodes includes B1, B2, and C3. The algorithm continues searching in this way until the search goal node is reached.

glossary.definition: A type of data-driven search where the search process starts from a root node and proceeds in the forward direction in the state space graph.
glossary.sentence: The search process starts from a root node and it proceeds in forward direction in the state space graph.

glossary.definition: A type of goal-driven search where the search process starts from a leaf node and proceeds in the backward direction in the state space graph.
glossary.sentence: The search process starts from a leaf node and it proceeds in backward direction in the state space graph.

glossary.definition: A sequence of nodes in the state space graph that represents a potential solution to be found by the search algorithm.
glossary.sentence: The search algorithm will first generate the path A->B->F->J. It will determine that this path does not lead to the search goal node, and the search algorithm will continue expanding nodes and will generate the path A->B->G->K.

glossary.definition: A strategy used by search algorithms to systematically explore different paths in a state space graph by revisiting previously explored nodes when necessary.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, it begins at the search start node and pursues a path until (Luger, 2009) : it reaches the search goal node, then it stops and returns the solution path; it reaches a leaf node that is not the search goal node, then it backtracks to the most recent node on the current path having unexamined descendants/ancestors (depending on the search direction) and continues searching.

glossary.definition: A sequence of nodes in a state space graph that revisits previously explored nodes, potentially causing the search algorithm to get stuck or loop indefinitely.
glossary.sentence: In this case, the search algorithm can get stuck in the cycle and never find the search goal node. Thus it would be logical to prevent the search algorithm from entering cycles, even though they are allowed in the state space graph.

glossary.definition: A category of search algorithms that use only the structure of a state space graph (nodes and arcs) without any additional information about node quality or costs to reach the search goal.
glossary.sentence: In general, there are two main categories of search algorithms (Figure 1): Uninformed search algorithms use only the state space graph or, in other words, its nodes and arcs. This kind of search is also called brute force search or blind search, because these algorithms explore the state space graph sequentially.

glossary.definition: A method used in intelligent computer systems for problems that may have an exact solution, but there are not enough computational resources to find that solution. It is often used when the state space graph representing the problem is really huge.
glossary.sentence: In any case, a heuristic evaluation function is specific to a problem because each problem has specific heuristic knowledge associated with it.

glossary.definition: A graph traversal algorithm that explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking.
glossary.sentence: The title of the algorithm reveals its main idea (Figure 10): “depth” means to go deeper and deeper into the state space graph while expanding nodes; “limited” indicates that there is a depth limit that defines how deeply the state space graph can be explored.

glossary.definition: A modification of Depth-First Search that overcomes a drawback related to going along indefinitely deep paths by setting a depth limit for exploration.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm takes the node located on the first position in the OPEN list - the node A - and checks if it is the search goal node. It is not the search goal node. The algorithm checks if its depth is lower than the depth limit.

glossary.definition: An uninformed search algorithm that explores all nodes at a given depth level before moving on to deeper levels, ensuring that every node is considered at least once and potentially finding the shortest path in an unweighted graph.
glossary.sentence: In this course, we will consider Depth-First Search, Depth-Limited Search, Breadth-First Search, and Bidirectional Search as examples of algorithms in the Uninformed search category.

glossary.definition: A search algorithm that consumes less computational resources than Breadth-First Search by expanding nodes from both the start and goal directions simultaneously. It can find the shortest path to the search goal node but has limited usage because it requires knowing the exact search goal node, which is often impossible in practical applications.
glossary.sentence: Bidirectional Search consumes less computational resources than Breadth-First Search.

glossary.definition: The maximum depth of exploration in Depth-Limited Search, determining how far into the state space graph nodes will be considered.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm does not consider nodes located below the defined depth limit.

glossary.definition: A list used by a search-based game algorithm to store nodes that have been evaluated but not yet expanded, sorted based on heuristic measures.
glossary.sentence: They are added to the OPEN list together with their heuristic measures in an ordered way. The algorithm checks if the search goal node exists among the nodes located in the OPEN list. The search goal node is found; thus, the algorithm stops its operation.

glossary.definition: A list used by a search-based game algorithm to store nodes that have been expanded and evaluated, preventing them from being re-evaluated.
glossary.sentence: The nodes E and H are moved to the CLOSED list as already expanded nodes. The nodes I and J are also moved to the CLOSED list as generated nodes, but they are not taken as the best nodes.

glossary.definition: A search algorithm that explores a graph level-by-level, where each node's children are explored before moving on to the next node.
glossary.sentence: The difference between Depth-First Search and Breadth-First Search is evident if we compare both ways of exploring the state space graph.

glossary.definition: A search algorithm that explores a graph level-by-level, where each node's children are explored before moving on to the next node. The first data element entered is the first to be processed.
glossary.sentence: The difference between Breadth-First Search and Bidirectional Search is evident if we use the analogy of the state space graph and tree crown (Figure 21).

glossary.definition: Knowledge used in the development of intelligent computer systems for problems that may have an exact solution, but there are not enough computational resources to find that solution. It is often used when the state space graph representing the problem is really huge.
glossary.sentence: In any case, a heuristic evaluation function is specific to a problem because each problem has specific heuristic knowledge associated with it.

glossary.definition: A function used in decision-making and game theory to evaluate the desirability or quality of a particular state, based on domain-specific knowledge or heuristics.
glossary.sentence: Iterative deepening explores the partial state space graph in an iterative manner – at each iteration, it goes one level deeper (Rich et al., 2009). It starts with depth limit of 1, and evaluates nodes at this depth using a heuristic evaluation function.

glossary.definition: A value or estimate used to evaluate the desirability of a particular state in a game or decision-making process.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm is able to assign only an intermediate heuristic measure of >=−1, since the node is located at the MAX level and the only known heuristic measure is a measure of −1.

glossary.definition: A game where factors such as the number of tiles out of place, sum of distances, and number of pairs of tile reversals can be used to evaluate nodes in a state space graph.
glossary.sentence: Another example is the game of 8-puzzle. There are 8 numbered tiles and 9 squares.

glossary.definition: The average number of arcs per node in the state space graph. It is calculated by dividing the sum of direct descendants of all nodes by the total number of nodes in the state space graph.
glossary.sentence: In any case, a heuristic evaluation function is specific to a problem because each problem has specific heuristic knowledge associated with it.

glossary.definition: The length of the longest path in the state space graph. It represents how deep the search can go into the graph before reaching an end.
glossary.sentence: In any case, a heuristic evaluation function is specific to a problem because each problem has specific heuristic knowledge associated with it.

glossary.definition: A phenomenon in game theory and decision-making where a limited depth of analysis (e.g., N-ply look-ahead) may lead to suboptimal decisions due to the inability to detect heuristically promising paths that are not within the limited search horizon.
glossary.sentence: One of the most significant problems with this approach is called the horizon effect (Jones, 2009; Luger, 2009). When a heuristic evaluation function is applied with limited look-ahead, it is possible that the depth of the look-ahead may not detect a heuristically promising path, and this may lead to a bad situation later in the game.

glossary.definition: A state space graph that can be fully stored and processed by an intelligent computer system, typically used in planning problems before a robot starts acting in some environment.
glossary.sentence: In any case, a heuristic evaluation function is specific to a problem because each problem has specific heuristic knowledge associated with it.

glossary.definition: A partially stored and processed state space graph due to limited computational resources. The search algorithm generates the graph up to a specified depth, using the heuristic evaluation function to choose nodes that are most likely to lead to the search goal node.
glossary.sentence: In any case, a heuristic evaluation function is specific to a problem because each problem has specific heuristic knowledge associated with it.

glossary.definition: A method used to transform the state space graph before its use by an intelligent computer system, reducing the number of states in the game tree and making the search more efficient. It is not always possible to apply this transformation.
glossary.sentence: In any case, a heuristic evaluation function is specific to a problem because each problem has specific heuristic knowledge associated with it.
Heuristic search algorithms differ both in the inputs that they need and the operational steps they perform (Figure 2).

They receive the search start node and the search goal node as input data, the state space graph, a data structure, is used to search for a problem solution, and they provide a solution path as their output.

The idea of the Hill Climbing algorithm is to always move to the best node in the state space graph. If we imagine that the state space graph with heuristic measures of nodes is similar to a mountain, then the search goal node can be considered to be the top of the mountain, while the search start node is the foot of the mountain (Figure 4).
The Best-First Search algorithm does not have these shortcomings.

glossary.definition: A search algorithm that selects a limited number of best nodes for further expansion in each iteration, providing a compromise between Hill Climbing and Best-First Search.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm always simultaneously expands all nodes located in the OPEN list at each iteration, evaluates their descendants/ancestors (depending on a search direction), and chooses the best nodes from the generated nodes according to the beam width.

glossary.definition: The number of best nodes selected by a search-based game algorithm based on heuristic measures, used to improve the efficiency of the algorithm.
glossary.sentence: According to the defined beam width, the algorithm selects the two best nodes based on heuristic measures. These are nodes K and F. They are added to the OPEN list together with their heuristic measures in an ordered way.

glossary.definition: A state space graph that includes all states from the initial state of a game to all goal states, used by intelligent computer systems with sufficient computational resources.
glossary.sentence: A complete state space graph is typically used in simple games with a small number of states, or when sufficient computational resources are available to store and process the state space graph during the operation of the intelligent computer system.

glossary.definition: A simplified or incomplete representation of all possible states in a game or decision-making process, used to reduce computational complexity.
glossary.sentence: Let’s consider these strategies. A partial state space graph is a more complex case, since the results of the game cannot be evaluated at the terminal nodes of the sub-graph.

glossary.definition: A player whose goal is to minimize the game result for the other player.
glossary.sentence: the task of the minimiser is to minimise the game result for the maximiser

glossary.definition: Values assigned to leaf nodes representing the outcome of a specific node in the state space graph
glossary.sentence: It involves the following steps (Luger, 2009): create a complete state space graph; label the levels of the graph as MIN and MAX; assign heuristic measures to all leaf nodes according to an analysis of who wins at a specific node; propagate heuristic measures of leaf nodes up to the root node of the state space graph according to the following rules: a) if a node is located at MAX level, it acquires the maximum heuristic measure from among its direct descendants, and b) if a node is located at MIN level, it acquires the minimum heuristic measure from among its direct descendants; determine the result of the game and the winning paths.

glossary.definition: A level in the state space graph representing the minimising player's turn
glossary.sentence: It involves the following steps (Luger, 2009): create a complete state space graph; label the levels of the graph as MIN and MAX; assign heuristic measures to all leaf nodes according to an analysis of who wins at a specific node; propagate heuristic measures of leaf nodes up to the root node of the state space graph according to the following rules: a) if a node is located at MAX level, it acquires the maximum heuristic measure from among its direct descendants, and b) if a node is located at MIN level, it acquires the minimum heuristic measure from among its direct descendants; determine the result of the game and the winning paths.

glossary.definition: A level in the state space graph representing the maximising player's turn
glossary.sentence: It involves the following steps (Luger, 2009): create a complete state space graph; label the levels of the graph as MIN and MAX; assign heuristic measures to all leaf nodes according to an analysis of who wins at a specific node; propagate heuristic measures of leaf nodes up to the root node of the state space graph according to the following rules: a) if a node is located at MAX level, it acquires the maximum heuristic measure from among its direct descendants, and b) if a node is located at MIN level, it acquires the minimum heuristic measure from among its direct descendants; determine the result of the game and the winning paths.

glossary.definition: To pass on or transmit (heuristic measures) up to a higher level in the state space graph
glossary.sentence: It involves the following steps (Luger, 2009): create a complete state space graph; label the levels of the graph as MIN and MAX; assign heuristic measures to all leaf nodes according to an analysis of who wins at a specific node; propagate heuristic measures of leaf nodes up to the root node of the state space graph according to the following rules: a) if a node is located at MAX level, it acquires the maximum heuristic measure from among its direct descendants, and b) if a node is located at MIN level, it acquires the minimum heuristic measure from among its direct descendants; determine the result of the game and the winning paths.

glossary.definition: Nodes that are directly connected to a specific node in the state space graph
glossary.sentence: It involves the following steps (Luger, 2009): create a complete state space graph; label the levels of the graph as MIN and MAX; assign heuristic measures to all leaf nodes according to an analysis of who wins at a specific node; propagate heuristic measures of leaf nodes up to the root node of the state space graph according to the following rules: a) if a node is located at MAX level, it acquires the maximum heuristic measure from among its direct descendants, and b) if a node is located at MIN level, it acquires the minimum heuristic measure from among its direct descendants; determine the result of the game and the winning paths.

glossary.definition: The scenario where a player's strategy results in the least favorable outcome.

glossary.definition: The scenario where a player's strategy results in the most favorable outcome.

glossary.definition: A process where a branch of the search tree is cut off because it will not affect the final decision.

glossary.definition: A process where a branch of the search tree is cut off because it will not affect the final decision.
glossary.sentence: a β-cut-off should be performed by cutting off the unconsidered paths below the current node

glossary.definition: A sequence of moves or decisions made by a player in a game.
glossary.sentence: it checks whether the ancestor of the current node along the current path also has an intermediate heuristic measure

glossary.definition: The node that is being evaluated at the moment in the search tree.
